notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s what stands out in your recurring charges with your current goals in mind (Emergency fund $200, Car $1,000).

1. Quick snapshot

You’ve got a mix of small subscriptions and essential bills:

Washington State DOT – $11.67 biweekly (tolls/transit, usage-based)
Chegg – $17.15/month (study/education, last paid 2023-04-24)
CenturyLink – $63.75/month (internet/phone, last paid 2022-08-16)
Apple – $2.96/month (small digital subscription)
Seattle City Light – $35.83/month (electric, last paid 2023-10-24)
2. Easiest wins to free up cash

A. CenturyLink – $63.75/month (Internet & Cable budget is $120/month)
This one’s the biggie.

Last paid in 2022, but it’s still showing as a recurring stream.
It’s likely either:
A legacy bill that’s not actually active anymore, or
A service still getting billed on an old account/card that hasn’t hit your main accounts recently.

You could:

Log into your CenturyLink account and confirm if anything is still active.
If inactive: delete the recurring stream in Monarch so it stops cluttering things.
If active but unused (old address, duplicate internet): cancel the service.

If it’s still active and you cancel, that’s about $64/month back in your pocket.

B. Chegg – $17.15/month
Last paid April 2023, so this has “forgotten subscription” vibes if you’re not using it.

You could:

Check your Chegg account and billing history.
If you only need it sometimes:
Cancel and re-subscribe during heavy-use months, or
Downgrade to a cheaper tier if they offer one.

If cancelled, that’s about $17/month saved.

C. Apple – $2.96/month
Small charge, but over a year it still adds up.

You could:

On your Apple device: go to Settings → [Your Name] → Subscriptions.
See what this is and ask:
Does it support something important (photos backup, a key app, etc.)?
If not, cancel or switch to a free/cheaper option.

If cancelled, that’s about $3/month saved.

3. Essential but worth keeping an eye on

Washington State Department of Transportation – $11.67 biweekly (~$25/month)
Likely tolls or transit, so this is usage-based.

You could:

Treat it as part of your transportation budget.
If you want more for your Emergency fund or Car goals, consider:
Carpooling, alternate routes, or public transit to cut tolls.

Seattle City Light – $35.83/month
This is an essential utility, so not something to cancel.

You could:

Ask about budget billing or any low‑income/efficiency programs.
Try small usage tweaks (LED bulbs, thermostat changes, unplugging devices).

Savings here are smaller and variable, but even $5–10/month helps.

4. How this helps your goals

If CenturyLink and Chegg are truly unused and you cancel them, and you also drop the Apple subscription:

Total potential monthly savings:
CenturyLink: ~$64
Chegg: ~$17
Apple: ~$3
Total ≈ $84/month

Redirected to your goals:

Emergency fund $200: fully funded in about 3 months.
Car goal $1,000: after that, the same $84/month gets you to $1,000 in about 12 more months.

Bottom line: Cleaning up just a few subscriptions could fully cover your emergency fund and make steady progress on the car without changing anything else.

Merchant	Frequency	Amount	Comment	Potential Action	Est. Monthly Savings
Washington State DOT	Biweekly	$11.67	Likely tolls/transit, usage-based	Only change if usage can drop	Varies
Chegg	Monthly	$17.15	Study/education subscription, last paid 2023-04-24	Cancel if no longer used	$17
CenturyLink	Monthly	$63.75	Internet/phone, last paid 2022-08-16	Confirm if still active, cancel if not	$64
Apple	Monthly	$2.96	Small digital subscription (iCloud, app, etc.)	Keep or cancel based on value	$3
Seattle City Light	Monthly	$35.83	Electric utility, last paid 2023-10-24	Essential, but can reduce usage	Small/variable
